title: AI-Orchestrated Human Microgeneration System — Patent & Design Notes
content:
# AI-Orchestrated Human Microgeneration System — Patent & Design Notes

Status: Authoritative / Patent Source
Created: 2026-01-04
Purpose: Formal invention capture for provisional/non-provisional patent application
1. Invention Summary (Authoritative)
This invention describes an AI-controlled energy orchestration system in which human-powered devices are treated as stochastic, influenceable energy resources. Machine-learning models predict human-generated energy output and dynamically control energy convergence, routing, and behavioral feedback mechanisms to optimize stability, utilization, and safety.
Key Novelty: Humans as influenceable energy resources inside the control loop — not passive generators, but active participants whose behavior is modulated by the system to achieve grid-level objectives.
2. Control Problem Definition
Inputs
Electrical telemetry (voltage, current, power)
Behavioral telemetry (effort, cadence, resistance)
Contextual signals (time, occupancy, tariffs)
Outputs
Power routing decisions (to loads, storage, grid)
Resistance and incentive modulation (behavioral control signals)
Objectives
Energy stability
Net utilization/export
User safety and comfort
Grid compliance
3. System Architecture Blocks
Human-Powered Generation Nodes
Role: Convert human kinetic energy to electrical power
Inputs: Human physical effort
Outputs: DC electrical power, behavioral telemetry
Novelty: Treated as stochastic, controllable resources (not fixed generators)
Telemetry & Sensing Layer
Role: Collect real-time electrical and behavioral data
Inputs: Voltage, current, power, effort, cadence, resistance settings
Outputs: Structured telemetry streams to AI engine
Novelty: Dual-domain sensing (electrical + behavioral) enables predictive control
AI Orchestration Engine
Role: Predict human energy output and optimize system-wide control decisions
Inputs: Historical and real-time telemetry, contextual signals
Outputs: Routing commands, resistance modulation signals, incentive triggers
Novelty: Machine learning models treat human output as predictable and influenceable
Energy Convergence Layer
Role: Aggregate power from multiple human-powered nodes into unified DC bus
Inputs: Variable DC power from generation nodes
Outputs: Stable, controllable DC bus
Novelty: Dynamic convergence control (not passive bus bar)
Distribution & Routing Layer
Role: Allocate converged power to loads, storage, and grid based on AI decisions
Inputs: DC bus power, AI routing commands
Outputs: Power to multiple endpoints
Novelty: AI-driven dynamic allocation (not fixed priority)
Human Feedback Interfaces
Role: Modulate human behavior through resistance, incentives, and visual indicators
Inputs: AI control signals
Outputs: Resistance changes, gamification rewards, grid stability displays
Novelty: Behavioral modulation as first-class control signal, not UI feature
4. Behavioral Feedback as Control Signals
Critical Patent Element: Behavioral modulation is a first-class control signal, not a UI feature.
Resistance Modulation
AI adjusts mechanical resistance on human-powered devices
Increases resistance when surplus power available (encourages more effort)
Decreases resistance when power needed elsewhere (encourages sustained contribution)
Incentives / Gamification
AI triggers rewards based on grid stability objectives
Points, achievements, or monetary incentives tied to optimization goals
Predictive models anticipate behavioral response to incentive structures
Visual Grid Stability Indicators
Real-time display of grid health to human participants
Encourages voluntary behavioral changes aligned with system objectives
Provides transparency into AI decision-making
Enablement Note: These mechanisms form a closed feedback loop where human behavior influences energy output, AI predicts and optimizes this behavior, and system adjusts to achieve objectives.
5. Claim Strategy Notes (Private)
Independent Claim Must Include:
AI prediction of human-generated energy output
Dynamic convergence/routing of aggregated power
Behavioral modulation as control mechanism
Fallback Dependent Claim Ideas
Specific ML model types (LSTM, transformer for time-series prediction)
Resistance modulation via electronically controlled magnetic/friction systems
Multi-objective optimization (stability + export + comfort)
Integration with external grid tariff signals
Safety enforcement layer (physiological limits, device constraints)
Specific incentive structures (gamification, monetary, environmental)
Prior Art Differentiation
Not just energy harvesting (no AI prediction or behavioral control)
Not just smart grid (human generators are not typical distributed resources)
Not just fitness tracking (no energy orchestration or convergence)
Not abstract AI (specific technical problem: energy stability with stochastic human inputs)
6. Implementation References
Existing AI orchestration workflows may serve as example embodiments of the AI orchestration engine without disclosure of proprietary source code.
Reference Projects:
AI Workflow Architect: Multi-AI coordination and orchestration protocols
6 Sacred Languages Protocol: Encrypted coordination messaging
Email AI Backboard: Resilient message-passing infrastructure
GitHub automation workflows: Event-driven orchestration patterns
Note: These references demonstrate feasibility and enablement without exposing trade secrets. Patent application can cite "AI orchestration systems known in the art" while the specific implementation remains proprietary.
Next Steps
Immediate Actions
Link Figma diagrams (System Architecture, Control Flow)
Review and refine claim strategy
Identify any additional enablement gaps
Next Iteration Options
Draft 1 independent + 6 dependent claims
Stress-test against §103 obviousness
Produce non-provisional-ready diagram set
Align with GitHub workflows for enablement language
Conduct prior art search
Prepare provisional filing materials
Related Pages
Patent Resources:
Link to Figma System Architecture Diagram (add when available)
Link to Figma Control Flow Diagram (add when available)
Technical References:
AI Workflow Architect documentation
Existing orchestration system implementations
